generalCitation: Civil Code of Quebec, CQLR c CCQ-1991, Art. 2925

credit-card.notes: Quebec's 3-year prescription period applies to most personal debts including credit cards. The clock starts from when the creditor could have first brought a claim (typically the first missed payment or default date).
credit-card.citation: Civil Code of Quebec, CQLR c CCQ-1991, Art. 2925

medical.notes: Medical debts owed to private providers follow the 3-year prescription. Debts to public health institutions may have specific rules — verify with a Quebec consumer protection lawyer.
medical.citation: Civil Code of Quebec, CQLR c CCQ-1991, Art. 2925

auto-loan.notes: Auto loan deficiency balances in Quebec are subject to the 3-year civil prescription period from the date of the deficiency.
auto-loan.citation: Civil Code of Quebec, CQLR c CCQ-1991, Art. 2925

personal-loan.notes: Personal loans are subject to Quebec's 3-year prescription period. Any acknowledgement of debt (written or payment) can restart the clock.
personal-loan.citation: Civil Code of Quebec, CQLR c CCQ-1991, Art. 2925

student-loan.notes: Private student loans follow the 3-year prescription. Provincial AFE loans and federal Canada Student Loans may have separate timelines.
student-loan.citation: Civil Code of Quebec, CQLR c CCQ-1991, Art. 2925

mortgage-deficiency.notes: Mortgage deficiency claims in Quebec are generally subject to the 3-year prescription under the Civil Code, though hypothec (mortgage) actions may have different timelines. Consult a notary or attorney.
mortgage-deficiency.citation: Civil Code of Quebec, CQLR c CCQ-1991, Art. 2925 and Art. 2923

utility-bill.notes: Utility bill debts are subject to Quebec's standard 3-year prescription period.
utility-bill.citation: Civil Code of Quebec, CQLR c CCQ-1991, Art. 2925

regulatoryBody: Office de la protection du consommateur (OPC)
complaints: File a complaint with the OPC at opc.gouv.qc.ca or call 1-888-672-2556. Collection agencies are regulated under the Consumer Protection Act, CQLR c P-40.1.
